The Shoemaker's Elves made the case that the 12 to 14 hours you aren't working each day are","title":"The Elves Are Open Source","cover_media":"3_2035726295670104064","plain_text":"Over the past month I've written about a system I use to get months of development work done while I sleep. The Shoemaker's Elves made the case that the 12 to 14 hours you aren't working each day are now a productive resource. The Survival Guide described the documents that keep an agent on track during long unsupervised runs. Water the Tree explained how the Ralph Loop is far more general than most people realize, and how central it is to effectively working with modern AI.
People kept asking the same question: can you share the actual system?
Today I'm open-sourcing it.
The repo is at github.com/aigorahub/elves. MIT license. Works with Claude Code, Codex, and any platform that supports the Agent Skills standard.
What it feels like
I'm not describing this from a quiet room with nothing else to do. I'm CEO of Aigora, which means sales calls, client meetings, admin, marketing, hiring, and everything else that comes with running a company. I teach a 10-week AI course. I host AigoraCast, an industry podcast. I publish papers and speak at conferences. I'm also trying to stay current on AI advances and implement them across our products, which in this environment is practically a full-time job by itself. On top of all that, I'm a father to two young, high-energy kids with ADHD, I'm trying to be a good husband, and I'm trying to stay in decent shape. It's a lot. My schedule is chaos. Yours probably is too.
That's the point. The elves fit into a chaotic life, not a clean one.
I run multiple projects at the same time. Different apps, different codebases, different clients. Each one has its own plan, its own survival guide, its own execution log. Each one has elves working on it.
Planning and review happen in the gaps. Between calls I'll spend 20 minutes reviewing what an overnight run produced. Over lunch I'll write a plan for the next batch and kick off a run. The agents work while I'm on a sales call, while I'm recording a podcast episode, while I'm prepping slides for a conference talk. My job is to keep them aimed at the right problems and to review what they produce.
The result is that I singlehandedly built Theus, an enterprise AI platform that's now in production at multiple Fortune 500 companies. And across Aigora more broadly, our productivity has gone through the roof as our other two developers have started implementing their own versions of the system. I don't think there's anything unusual about me that makes this work. I think the system is what makes it work, and I think most people could get similar leverage from it once they learn the rhythm.
In my own use, I've gotten six to nine months of equivalent development work done in roughly three to four hours of my total time per project, spread across planning, check-ins, and final review. Multiply that across several projects running simultaneously and the math gets striking.
This isn't about reclaiming your evenings. You might do that, and that's fine. But what I've found is that once you have the system working, the constraint isn't time anymore. The constraint is knowing what to build next. The elves are always ready. The bottleneck is you deciding what matters.
That said, there have been stretches where serious work was shipping in the background while I was in meetings, or on a date with my wife, or playing with my kids. That part is real. The elves don't care whether you're working on another project or not working at all. They just keep going.
What it is
Elves is an Agent Skill. You install it, start a planning conversation with your agent, and kick off a run that can last hours or days. The planning takes about 30 minutes. You and the agent talk through what you're building, break it into batches, set the non-negotiables, configure the test gates, and agree on when you'll be back.
Then the agent runs. For each batch, it implements the code, runs the tests, reads the PR review comments, fixes what the reviewers found, and iterates until the batch is tight. Then it moves to the next batch. Each batch stands on the verified foundation of the one before it.
The batch size should match what the model can get right with high confidence. For current frontier models, that's roughly what a team of four developers would accomplish in a two-week sprint. But you define the sprint size based on your model and your stack. Codex can push larger batches than Claude in some codebases. You tune it over time.
A plan might have twelve of those batches. Twelve sprints chained together overnight is months of human-team output by morning.
The review step is where the Ralph Loop does its real work. After each batch, the agent reads the PR comments from bots, CI, or humans, fixes blocking issues, pushes, and iterates until the batch is clean. The review isn't something that accumulates for you to read in the morning. The review is part of the agent's loop. By the time you see the work, it has already been reviewed and refined, possibly multiple times.
Then you spend 30 minutes reviewing the result. Your review is a final pass on work that's already tight, not a first look at raw output.
How it works
Three documents persist across the entire run.
The Plan is what the human writes. It describes the work and breaks it into batches. The agent treats it as the source of truth.
The Survival Guide is the standing brief. It contains the mission, the non-negotiables, the current phase, the next batch, and the tool configuration. After every push, the agent re-reads it. After every context compaction or restart, it reads it first. This is what keeps the agent anchored when its conversation memory gets wiped.
The Execution Log is the running record. Every batch gets a timestamped entry: what changed, what commands ran, what the tests said, what the review found, what decisions were made, what the commit hash is, and what comes next. When you check in the next morning, you open the log and know exactly where things stand.
The skill also includes safety hardening for unattended runs: forbidden git commands (the agent can never run git reset --hard or git push --force), test integrity rules (the agent can never modify a test to make it pass), non-interactive operation (surveys, popups, and prompts are suppressed), and rollback tags before every batch.
The agent never merges. That gate stays with you.
What it isn't
This is v0. The system I use in production at Aigora is more elaborate than what you see here. It includes custom review tools, proprietary verification infrastructure, and integration with our internal deployment pipeline. I've extracted the key ideas and patterns into something that works with standard tools (git, GitHub PRs, CI) so it's useful to anyone, not just people with my exact setup. I'll be using this open-source version myself going forward, with my additional tooling bolted on, so it will continue to improve from real production use. But it hasn't been battle-tested across dozens of different projects, stacks, and models. It works well for me. It may need adjustment for you.
The skill is scaffolding, not a finished product. Every project is different. Your test commands, your review infrastructure, your non-negotiables, your batch sizing will all be specific to your situation. Elves gives you the framework. You customize it and learn your own lessons as you go.
The first time you run it overnight, you'll discover things no template can predict: which of your test suites is flaky, which commands prompt for input and need --yes flags, how long your batches actually take, where your plan was vague and the agent had to guess. That's normal. After each run, read the execution log and update your setup with what you learned. The skill gets better every time you use it because you get better at writing plans and configuring the harness.
You don't have to leave
You can ride along. Watch the agent work, check in, give it additional context, adjust priorities. But there's one rule: say "do not stop" in every message. Explicitly. Every time. It sounds redundant but it makes a measurable difference in whether the agent keeps working or pauses to have a conversation.
The big picture
If you haven't read the earlier articles, here's the short version.
Most people treat AI like a calculator. You type a prompt, get an answer, and it's either right or wrong. That's the wrong frame. AI returns drafts, not answers. The right frame is the Ralph Loop: try, check, feed back, repeat. A dumb, stubborn loop beats over-engineered sophistication because any single attempt might fail, but if you keep iterating, the process converges.
What makes this moment different from every previous era of automation is that generative AI expanded what computers can evaluate. The loop can now include text, images, video, audio, and even value judgments in the evaluation step. That's why the pattern applies to almost any kind of work, not just code.
The human's job is the bread of the sandwich. On the front end, you decide what's worth building and specify the problem. On the back end, you review the output and exercise judgment. In the middle, the agent runs Ralph Loops, mostly while you sleep. Elves is the harness that makes the middle run for hours without supervision.
What I need from you
I'm sharing what works for me. I need your help making it work for more people.
If you try Elves and something doesn't work, open an issue. If you run an overnight session, tell me how it went. There's an Overnight Run Report template in the issue tracker for exactly this purpose. What worked? What broke? What did you change in your setup afterward?
I especially want to hear about how well the built-in review works without additional reviewer bots, whether the batch sizing guidance holds across different models and stacks, which parts of the preflight checklist catch real problems, and where the skill breaks on languages and frameworks I haven't tested yet.
The link
github.com/aigorahub/elves
MIT licensed. Works with Claude Code, Codex, Claude.ai, and any Agent Skills compatible platform. Install it, plan your first session, and let them work while you sleep.
Block the time. Brief the elves. Go live your life.
The work will be there in the morning.
This is the fourth article in a series: The Shoemaker's Elves (the 14-hour resource), The Survival Guide (keeping agents on track), Water the Tree (the Ralph Loop), and now this."}
